entry.title: Enabling Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) by SAP solution capabilities
entry.content:
This article intends to give an overview what SAP solutions enable the value chain for Carbon Capture & Storage. It’s meant to be an outside-in view and food for thought.

Enabling Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) by SAP solution capabilities for Energy companies: An Overview 

Global warming is one of the most urgent challenges of our time. Over the past century, average global temperatures have risen significantly, driven primarily by the accumulation of greenhouse gases (GHGs) in the atmosphere. Among this, carbon dioxide (CO₂) is the most influential due to its volume of emissions and its long atmospheric lifetime.

Despite measures to reduce energy consumption, CO2 emissions from industrial operations, transport, and households keep rising. While expectations for reduction are high – supported by electrification and renewable energies like wind, solar, and green H2 – certain "hard-to-abate" industries such as cement, iron, and steel face significant hurdles in reducing emissions effectively and efficiently. For these industries, where emissions are unavoidable, capturing CO₂ directly at the source and securely storing it underground helps stop it from entering the atmosphere and plays a vital role in emissions reduction.

Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) has evolved from experimental pilot projects into operational infrastructure. Recent years have witnessed breakthrough developments across the entire CCS value chain, from next-generation capture technologies achieving 95%+ efficiency to cross-border transport networks and proven geological storage at commercial scale.

Key global storage projects now demonstrate the viability of CCS:

  • Norway: The Northern Lights project has established Europe's first open-source CO₂ storage infrastructure, accepting emissions from multiple industrial emitters.
  • United States: The Petra Nova and Illinois Industrial CCS projects have validated large-scale capture from power and ethanol production.
  • Australia: The Gorgon project stores millions of tons annually in offshore formations.
  • United Kingdom: The Acorn and HyNet clusters are mobilizing industrial decarbonization at regional scale.

What is Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS)?

Carbon Capture & Storage is a comprehensive value chain that involves capturing CO2 emissions from industrial or energy-related sources, transporting it to a storage site, and permanently sequestering it in deep underground geological formations. These reservoirs often located in offshore subseafloor formations, ensure the carbon cannot contribute to atmospheric warming.

Typically, a CCS value chain consists of three major processes.

  1. Capture
    CO₂ is separated from exhaust gases using capture technologies. Modern efficiency often exceeds 90%.
  2. Transport
    Captured CO₂ is compressed and transported to the reservoir – typically via pipelines, but also by ship, truck, or rail. This often involves two transportation legs: First, from the capture location to a terminal (the handover from CO2 emitter to storage company). And second, from the terminal into the subsea reservoir.
  3. Storage
    CO₂ is injected into deep geological formations such as depleted oil and gas fields or saline aquifers. These formations are well characterized, have secure caprock layers, and have naturally held fluids and gases for millions of years.

Figure 1 shows how the value chain is modeled at Equinor in their Northern Lights CCS project.

Figure 1 - CCS value chain - Capture, Transport, Store (source: Equinor)

Current CCS projects share common attributes: integrated infrastructure connecting multiple emitters to shared storage, cross-party collaboration spanning industry and government, and sophisticated monitoring and verification systems. However, as CCS transitions from demonstration to deployment, a critical challenge emerges operational and digital integration.

 

Transport and Store as a service – A Business Model for oil & gas companies

In a full chain business model, a single company controls every stage of the CCS process across Capture, Transport and Storage. However, a decoupled model is emerging as the dominant approach for oil & gas Industry.

Oil & gas companies are uniquely positioned to deliver CCS storage solutions. They possess decades of experiences in subsurface engineering, largescale project execution, huge offshore infrastructure operations, seismic studies, reservoir modelling and bulk transportation of oil & gas. In addition, they have access to depleted reservoirs and understand their specific geological situations. Therefore, their focus is on transportation & storing of CO2, rather than on capturing it.  

We call the corresponding business model Transport and Store as a service:

Figure 2 - Transport and Store as a Service

CAPTURE

  • The oil & gas company is not involved in capturing CO2.
  • Emitting companies or specialized firms capture the CO2

TRANSPORT

  • Transport CO2 to a predefined transit point, typically a harbor or pipeline terminal.
  • At the transit point, handover and ownership change take place, and the oil & gas business model starts. The oil & gas company does bulk transportation from transit point to the subsurface reservoir.

STORE

  • Inject and store delivered CO2 quantity in subsurface reservoir.
  • Ensure E2E sustainability and safety compliance of reservoir and stored CO2.
  • Long-term monitoring and closure

In this service model, industrial customers pay a service fee per ton of CO2 delivered to the transit point. They will get a validated CO2 certificate for the delivered quantity.  

This model is now entering early commercial stages, as demonstrated by projects such as Northern Lights in Norway, HyNet in the UK, and Porthos in the Netherlands.
This paper focuses on this model, Transport & Storage as a Service, mapping the critical business capabilities and the enabling SAP S/4HANA solutions required to establish a unified digital core that connects operational reality to regulatory compliance and financial accounting.

Required Business Capabilities for Offering CCS Storage Services

To operate CCS at scale in a Transport & Store as a service business model, an oil and gas company needs to have strong capabilities across technical, commercial, operational, regulatory, and digital dimensions. Key business capabilities during revenue realization phase include:

 

Commercial & Customer Management

  • Customer onboarding and service delivery
  • Manage customer contracts across their lifecycle
  • Pricing and billing of CO₂ storage services

Transportation, Storage & Safety

  • Manage various types of transports
  • Quality & Inventory Management
  • Dangerous goods handling
  • CO₂ Injection
  • Monitoring, reporting and verification

Asset Lifecycle Management

  • Maintenance, reliability, and integrity
  • Large-scale project management
  • Procurement and logistics

Regulatory, Environmental & Compliance Management

  • Permitting and environmental impact assessments
  • Compliance with CO₂ storage regulations
  • Sustainability reporting and audits

Finance, Billing & JV Management

  • Capital project accounting
  • Multi‑party JV structures common in CCS hubs

 

Mapping CCS Business Capabilities to SAP S/4HANA

SAP S/4HANA and other SAP cloud solutions provide the digital foundation for CCS operators to follow the molecules along the value chain and to integrate financials, operations, logistics, compliance, and sustainability throughout the CCS service value chain.

Figure 3 SAP Solution Mapping

It all starts with aligning and setting up contracts with emitters in SAP S/4HANA Sales, with volume commitments, quality specifications and pricing formulas for delivering CO2. With that in place, emitters/customers can start nominating CO2 quantities for storing. SAP supports the complete contract-to-cash process, including logistics coordination and settlement and billing of CO2 quantities delivered, and the entire storage service.

SAP Hydrocarbon Supply and Primary Distribution solution manages transportation of CO2 by various means including vessels and pipelines. Transportation sourcing and inventory planning, schedule pipeline transport, execute the actual transport, manage tickets, claims and shipping costs.

SAP Quality Management enables organizations to plan quality requirements by specifications, and control characteristics for CO2 quality delivered, suppliers, and processes. The solution supports quality inspections throughout the supply chain, including goods receipt of CO2 and manages nonconformances through structured quality notifications that drive preventive actions. CO2 storage operators face a need for clear CO₂ quality specifications and contractual alignment with emitters and registering of injected volumes and composition.
For MRO, it is tightly integrated with procurement, inventory, and logistics, ensuring that quality decisions directly impact operational processes.

CO2 is a hazardous material. The SAP Environment, Health and Safety (SAP EHS) solution helps organizations manage environmental compliance, occupational health, and workplace safety within a single integrated system. The solution supports incident management, risk assessment, and preventive measures to reduce workplace hazards and improve employee safety all along the CO2 value chain. SAP EHS enables compliance with regulatory requirements by managing permits, emissions, hazardous substances, and safety data documentation. It provides structured processes for audits, inspections, and corrective actions, ensuring transparency and traceability. Integrated analytics and reporting deliver insights into EHS performance, supporting risk reduction, compliance assurance, and continuous improvement.

SAP Green Token enables companies to digitally track and allocate sustainability attributes, alongside physical material flows. It provides blockchain-based end‑to‑end transparency and auditable evidence of CO2 quantities and qualities in multi‑tier supply chains across capture, transport and storage processes. SAP Green Token supports credible sustainability claims by ensuring traceability, auditability, and compliance with certification and regulatory frameworks. By integrating with existing ERP and supply chain systems, it helps organizations operationalize communicate verified environmental impacts to customers and stakeholders.
SAP Advanced Analytics and SAP Sustainability Control Tower, SAP’s sustainability suite works together to enable measurement, governance, and value-chain transparency that underpin Carbon Capture and Storage programs. It centralizes ESG targets and performance, letting you track CCS as an abatement initiative, model scenarios, and report progress with audit-ready monitoring, reporting and verification (MRV). 
CCS storage operators must comply with geological storage permitting frameworks (such as the EU CCS Directive or EPA's Underground Injection Control Class VI rules in the US), which govern site selection, injection operations, and long-term CO₂ containment monitoring. High transparency, audit readiness, data management, and long-term record-keeping obligations are key, from quantities received to injected and eventually leaked to the surface.

In the process of CO2 transportation and storage, big technical installations and equipment are involved which require full attention to operate them efficiently and safely. SAP Enterprise Asset Management supervises asset health status, manages maintenance processes and orchestrates corresponding procurement and logistics for MRO materials.

  • SAP Enterprise Asset Management (EAM): Manages the entire lifecycle of CCS infrastructure, from pipelines to injection wells. It coordinates preventive and corrective maintenance to maximize the uptime and safety of these critical assets.
  • SAP Asset Performance Management (APM) uses sensor data (IoT) with maintenance history to assess asset health. By using predictive analytics, operators can prioritize interventions on offshore equipment, reducing unplanned downtime and environmental risks.
  • SAP Field Logistics: Tracks spare parts and materials across remote locations and offshore platforms, ensuring that technicians have the right tools available to minimize operational delays.

To scale Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) successfully, organizations must orchestrate massive volumes of operational data, maintain high-risk infrastructure, and ensure total compliance across the value chain. SAP Business AI meets this challenge by embedding intelligence directly into the SAP ecosystem, transforming raw data into automated, actionable insights.

Built on the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), this AI framework supports three critical pillars of CCS operations:

  • Supply Chain & Logistics Visibility: AI analyzes real-time logistics and market data to provide end-to-end visibility. For CCS, this means optimizing the midstream transport of compressed CO2 via ship, rail, or pipeline.
  • Intelligent Asset Management: By monitoring sensor data from capture plants and injection wells, AI identifies failure risks before they occur. This predictive maintenance is vital for CCS infrastructure, where downtime can lead to significant environmental and financial penalties.
  • The Rise of Joule Agents: SAP’s generative AI assistant, Joule, acts as an agent orchestrator. It allows operators to query complex CCS data using natural language, significantly reducing the manual effort required to manage global sequestration projects.
  • ESG What if scenarios: As CCS companies are a highly regulated industry means that they also need to report on their own emissions. SAP Sustainability Control Tower together with SAP Analytics Cloud serves as the central hub for ESG data. The addition of AI makes it possible to go beyond reporting but also looks into what if scenarios. What happens if we increase the injection by 15%, what would the effect on the emissions?

Conclusion

Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) is a crucial technology for decarbonization, particularly for industries with limited alternatives for emission reduction. Oil and gas companies are uniquely positioned to lead this transition due to their subsurface expertise and extensive offshore infrastructure. However, a successful CCS venture is more than a technical feat; it is a sophisticated service business that demands robust strategic, commercial, and digital capabilities. SAP S/4HANA provides the integrated solution landscape necessary to connect operational reality with logistics flows, regulatory compliance and financial integrity, creating a scalable foundation for the future of carbon management.

entry.title: Announcement: Register to the new CEI project for SAP Application Interface Framework
entry.content:
SAP Application Interface Framework (AIF) is an integral part of SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud for implementing, monitoring, and managing application interfaces.

In production environments, when a large number of interface messages fail, manual error diagnosis and error resolution can lead to significant effort and time. To address this, we plan to enhance SAP Application Interface Framework to support an AI-assisted error remediation framework that transforms error handling from tedious manual work into intelligent, automated recovery.

To gather feedback on those planned product improvements, we will run the Customer Engagement Initiative (CEI) project AI-Assisted Error Remediation for SAP Application Interface Framework. The CEI project aims to collect feedback on the concept and a first proof of concept at an early stage, focusing on:

  • Error classification: How AI-driven error classification can reduce manual effort.
  • Remediation automation: Validating the remediation approach against real customer scenarios.
  • Autonomous vs. guided remediation: Understanding customer preferences for automatic execution versus human-in-the-loop approval.

If you like to join the upcoming SAP Application Interface Framework influence project, please register here (link leads to the customer influence page).

Registration is open from May 18th until June 21st 2026.

Once, the registration closes, we will invite you for an initial call where we go through the scope and the prerequisites of the feedback project. This will be around end of June, begin of July, the actual feedback sessions will start in July.

We are looking forward to your active participation and valuable feedback.

entry.title: Como integrar um serviço BTP Cloud Foundry a um ambiente S4HANA On-premise
entry.content:
O cenário é o seguinte: você quer hospedar um app Fiori Freestyle no BTP Cloud Foundry, que vai se conectar a um sistema S4HANA on-premise e será utilizado pelos usuários finais.

Pode parecer bem específico, mas isso envolve um entendimento de várias camadas de arquitetura, então vale a pena tomar um tempo para entender isso.

Esse objetivo envolve criar serviços auxiliares, que vão se comunicar com o S4HANA por intermédio do Cloud Connector.

O primeiro passo é instalar o SAP Cloud Connector, pode ser no mesmo servidor que hospeda o S4HANA ou em outro servidor na mesma rede.

Ele é um programa Java, então é necessário instalar a JVM. Tecnicamente pode ser usada qualquer JVM, porém a SAP também possui suas próprias versões da JVM, então é recomendável usar elas. Os detalhes estão nesse link: https://help.sap.com/docs/connectivity/sap-btp-connectivity-cf/installation.

Com ele rodando no ambiente, você vai configurar os dados da sua BTP subaccount (ele pode se conectar em mais de uma subaccount).

A conexão entre os serviços da subaccount e o S4HANA vão ser intermediadas por essa conexão com o Cloud Connector.

Quando falamos em integração de serviços externos com um ambiente SAP, não podemos ignorar a parte de autenticação e segurança. Existem várias formas de configuração, mas a mais “nativa” para esse cenário seria a Principal Propagation.

Para entender a arquitetura, mentalize o seguinte fluxo, supondo que no Cloud Foundry você escolha o Node como backend:

  • O usuário abre a aplicação Fiori, que tenta puxar uma informação de uma API do S4HANA através do path relativo (Ex: /sap/opu/odata4/sap/z*);

  • Essa requisição cai no container Node, e se o usuário não estiver logado, ele é redirecionado para uma url de autenticação, onde é informado seu email e senha do Identity Provider (que pode ser da SAP, Azure, AWS, etc.);

  • Essa autenticação gera um token com informações do usuário, que é enviado ao Node;

  • O Node, tendo o token e o caminho da API, direciona ambos para um destination, que aponta para a conexão criada no Cloud Connector;

  • O Cloud Connector pega uma informação desse token, por exemplo o e-mail do usuário, e gera um certificado digital temporário;

  • Esse certificado é enviado em uma conexão https para o endpoint real do S4HANA

  • O S4HANA valida o certificado e mapeia ele para um usuário interno

  • A partir daí a lógica da API odata segue normal, baseada no que o usuário pode fazer, e a resposta é devolvida;

Existe um outro blog post que também explica sobre o Principal Propagation. De qualquer forma vou explicar aqui, mas se quiser conferir, o link é: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/principal-propagation-setup-between-s4hana-and-btp-portal/ba-p/13522753.

Para tornar isso possível, após conectar o Cloud Connector na subaccount, o próximo passo vai ser configurar os certificados digitais dele, na área “Connector > Configuration > On-premises”.

Nesse painel existem 2 tipos de certificado digital: um é para o proxy do Cloud Connector, esse é denominado “System certificate”, e o outro é o que o Cloud Connector utiliza para gerar os certificados temporários, denominado “CA Certificate”.

Nessa mesma página é possível configurar quais informações extrair do token para gerar o certificado, na área do Principal Propagation.

Após isso, é hora de configurar a conexão com o S4HANA on-premise.

Primeiramente, o Cloud Connector precisa confiar no certificado https do servidor S4HANA. Por padrão ele só confia em uma lista definida de certificados, denominada “Back-end Trust Store”. Você pode fazer lá o upload manualmente, ou pode também desativar essa opção, assim ele vai confiar em qualquer certificado do back-end.

Feito isso, na área da sua subaccount, página “Cloud to On-Premises”, vai ser configurado os detalhes específicos do proxy.

A conexão deve ser necessariamente via HTTPS, pois é assim que o Cloud Connector consegue trocar o certificado gerado com o S4HANA de forma nativa através do próprio protocolo.

Exemplo (na edição ele exibe como HTTP, mas foi criada como HTTPS):

Feito isso, a próxima etapa é fazer o Cloud Connector confiar no token gerado pelo serviço de autenticação da sua subaccount.

Para isso, nessa mesma página do “Cloud to On-Premises” existe também a aba do “Principal propagation”, onde você deve sincronizar. A primeira execução é manual, depois dela você pode marcar a opção para sincronizar automaticamente.

 

Em resumo, até aqui já temos uma instância do Cloud Connector rodando na rede, vinculada a uma subaccount BTP, com os certificados system e CA criados, e configurada para se conectar ao S4HANA on-premise via https.

Agora faltam 2 coisas: configurar o S4HANA para confiar no Cloud Connector, e configurar os serviços no BTP Cloud Foundry para que seja possível essa integração.

Para que o S4HANA confie no Cloud Connector, é necessário ajustar alguns parâmetros de perfil do sistema (isso envolve configurações BASIS).

O primeiro ponto é fazer ele confiar no certificado digital de sistema do Cloud Connector, existem 2 formas de fazer isso:

  • Com os antigos parâmetros “icm/HTTPS/trust_client_with_subject” e “icm/HTTPS/trust_client_with_issuer”, que são configurados via transação RZ10. Esses parâmetros definem o padrão do emissor e assunto do certificado digital que o sistema pode confiar, e é um valor único. Eles são do tipo estático, então é necessário reiniciar a instância do S4 para aplicar as edições.

Exemplo:

  • Com o novo parâmetro “icm/trusted_reverse_proxy_<x>”, onde o “<x>” é um número sequencial começando do 0. Ele tem esse padrão porque dessa forma é possível configurar mais de um certificado confiado pelo servidor, diferente dos anteriores que só permitiam um valor. Ele é do tipo dinâmico, então não é sempre necessário reiniciar a instância para aplicar, e pode ser alterado a partir da transação RZ11 (de forma indireta). Mais informações sobre ele no link: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_NETWEAVER_AS_ABAP_752/077f95b579a0474fbef5d520be10b680/3bea0adde9a54db5843be8f7bf01fefa.html.

Exemplo:

Também é necessário importar os dois certificados do Cloud Connector na transação STRUST, no servidor SSL Padrão.

Feito isso, o sistema S4HANA confia no certificado do Cloud Connector, mas ainda não consegue mapear os certificados temporários para usuários dentro do SAP. Aí entra a configuração da transação CERTRULE, onde você faz o upload de um certificado temporário gerado pelo Cloud Connector e pode mapear ele para usuários através de regras, ou diretamente.

Exemplo:

Para habilitar isso também é necessário configurar um parâmetro de perfil, o “login/certificate_mapping_rulebased”:

No lado do S4HANA é basicamente isso, agora só falta configurar os serviços dentro do BTP.

O cenário pensado nesse tutorial é de uma aplicação CAP Fiori rodando no Cloud Foudry, então vamos criar os serviços no mesmo space da aplicação.

São basicamente 3 serviços:

  • destination: Conceito similar ao da transação SM59, guarda dentro dele o destino para a conexão do Cloud Connector. Ele possui painel próprio para configurações.

  • connectivity: É um serviço sem configuração nenhuma, que apenas serve como ponte entre a aplicação e o serviço de destination.

  • xsuaa: É o que faz a ponte da aplicação com o provedor de identidade. Ele é configurado apenas via json, onde é possível definir o app que vai consumi-lo, as urls que pode redirecionar após autenticar o usuário, e até mesmo criar novas roles dentro do BTP, que podem posteriormente ser incluídas em Role collections e atribuídas a usuários.

Feito isso, só falta configurar a conexão dentro do painel do “Destination Service”. Lá você vai criar um destination com o Proxy type “OnPremise” e mapear para a url virtual gerada no painel do Cloud Connector, com autenticação via “PrincipalPropagation”.

Exemplo:

O app node deve estar configurado para que o destino do S4HANA seja esse Name que você configurou acima (não confunda isso com a url virtual ou com o serviço destination).

Agora para integrar tudo isso, basta a aplicação estar configurada para fazer o binding nesses 3 serviços. Isso é feito através do arquivo “manifest.yml”, usado na hora de subir o app no Cloud Foundry.

Exemplo:

Finalmente, seu app Node estará pronto para se autenticar e consumir serviços odata do seu S4HANA On-Premise.

 

Em resumo, ao seguir esse tutorial você foi capaz de entender e configurar as camadas de integração entre um app no BTP Cloud Foundry e um sistema S4Hana On-Premise (via api OData).

Talvez essas ferramentas, configurações e caminhos específicos mudem com o tempo, o mais importante é entender a arquitetura e os conceitos envolvidos nessa integração.

Obrigado pela leitura, espero ter ajudado 😄

entry.title: AI Needs Clean Data. Business Networks Stop It Getting Dirty in the First Place
entry.content:
There is a concept in professional kitchens called mise en place, everything in its place before service begins. The best chefs do not start cooking and hope the ingredients sort themselves out mid-order. The prep happens first, deliberately and completely, so that when the pressure is on the kitchen can move at pace without chaos.

For some reason my brain decided to align that principle to the concept of data quality and its use in agentic AI. I finally figured out what caused the spark, Zero100's AI-Ready Data Imperative report which defines the material difference between data that informs a decision, data that triggers a recommendation, and data that an agent acts on autonomously (there is also a nice podcast on the topic). A dashboard built on imperfect data gets questioned by a human who applies judgement and context. An agent built on imperfect data acts, reroutes the shipment, adjusts the replenishment signal, commits to the supplier, before anyone has noticed the input was wrong. The same ambiguity that a person absorbs in a meeting becomes operational risk the moment a system is doing the acting.

The same research found that only 15% of organisations consider their data foundations execution-ready. Let that land for a moment, because that means 85% of the AI programmes being announced, funded, and celebrated right now are being built on ground that cannot support the weight being placed on them.

The honest reality is that the foundations have not been prepared for this level of trust, and most organisations are not yet having that conversation clearly enough to know it.

The instinctive response is to treat this as a reason to delay. Clean everything first, then deploy agents. That instinct is understandable, but most organisations do not have the luxury of acting on it. Board pressure is intense, Wall Street is rewarding AI narratives and punishing silence, and vendors are racing to rebadge everything as agentic. The result is a top-down mandate to move fast colliding with a bottom-up reality that the foundations are not ready. Waiting for the data estate to be fully clean is not a strategy, it is an indefinite deferral dressed up as diligence. I have yet to meet the enterprise that finished a data quality programme and declared victory.

So the real question is not how do we clean everything but what do we actually need to clean to make a specific decision trustworthy.

Zero100's research consistently finds that the list of data inputs that actually determine the outcome of any given decision is shorter than most people expect. Work backwards from the decision, inventory rebalancing, late shipment triage, supplier payment timing, and ask which fields, if wrong or late, make the agent's action wrong or late. Typically ten to twenty fields. They call these Golden Inputs, not because they are glamorous, but because they carry disproportionate weight and deserve disproportionate attention. Govern those fields with the same rigour applied to operational KPIs, and you have a foundation an agent can genuinely act on. Leave them in the same state as everything else, and you have an agent making fast, confident, wrong decisions.

Here is where the argument takes a turn that most conversations about data readiness miss entirely. In supply chain, the triggering event is almost always external. A supplier confirmation, a carrier delay, an invoice exception, a forecast revision. The decisions agents will be asked to make are not primarily driven by data that originates inside your four walls. They are driven by signals arriving from outside them. Which means the most consequential Golden Inputs in any supply chain decision are very often external data fields, not internal ones.

This matters because it reframes the entire data cleansing challenge. Internal data cleansing is valuable but it is a continuous battle, because dirty data keeps entering the estate faster than it can be cleaned. Every new transaction, every supplier update, every manual entry is a potential source of contamination. It is the culinary equivalent of trying to keep a kitchen clean while unchecked deliveries arrive throughout service.

Business networks change that dynamic fundamentally. Data arriving over a governed network is structured, validated, and clean at the point of entry. Those fields do not join the cleansing backlog because they never needed cleansing in the first place. Think of it as the difference between a kitchen that inspects every ingredient as it arrives from the supplier, and one that waits until service to discover the fish was not fresh. Quality is established before it enters the kitchen, not discovered as a problem halfway through cooking.

This is where SAP Business Network plays a defining role. With millions of trading partners already connected and transacting through structured, governed document flows, the network is delivering AI-ready Golden Inputs at scale today, not as a future state. Purchase orders, advance ship notices, invoices, confirmations, each carrying their critical fields, all arriving clean by design. For organisations building toward agentic AI in supply chain, that is not a nice-to-have, it is the shortest path between ambition and trustworthy autonomous action.

The path to agentic AI is not a transformation programme. It is a sequence of small, governed steps, each one earning the right to the next. Start with a single decision, identify its Golden Inputs, and rather than beginning an internal cleansing project of uncertain duration, ask which of those inputs arrives via a business network and is therefore already clean, already structured, already trustworthy. In supply chain that answer is often most of them.

The problem in most kitchens is not the quality of the chefs. It is that the ingredients keep arriving without quality checks, and the team spends half its time sorting through the delivery before it can start cooking. The best kitchens do not just prep well, they have rigorous supplier standards, so the ingredients arrive ready to use.

That is what business networks do for agentic AI. Not a better mop. A cleaner kitchen.

entry.title: Service Bindings with OAuth 2.0 for SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition
entry.content:
This post outlines the procedure to specify client credentials required for SAP S/4HANA or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition systems to communicate with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition, using OAuth 2.0 client authentication. OAuth 2.0 provides a secure, token-based approach that is required for newer integration scenarios with SAP BTP services.

Prerequisites

Before starting the procedure, ensure the following requirements are met:

  • Initial setup in SAP BTP has been performed manually or via the Set Up an Account for SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition booster
  • Required country/region-specific processes are active for your subaccount.
  • SAP Note 3540443 – Electronic Communication Integration Framework: Implementation Overview has been implemented.
  • You have the Space Developer or Org Manager role in your SAP BTP subaccount.

Note: If initial setup for a process or business system is already complete, it does not need to be repeated for additional processes in the same subaccount region.

Step 1: Create the Service Binding

    • Navigate to the Subaccount — In the SAP BTP cockpit, go to the subaccount where the service binding will be created.

       

    • Access Instances and Subscriptions: Choose Services > Instances and Subscriptions.

 

    • Select Service Instance: Select the service instance created during the initial setup of the cloud edition.

 

    • Create Service Binding: In the Service Bindings section on the right, choose Create and fill in the fields as follows:

 

  • Finalize: Choose Create.

Special Instructions for France (E-Invoices and E-Reports)

If integrating to activate the E-Invoices and E-Reports (France) process, repeat the steps above but select 'Peppol Exchange' as the process instead of 'Other Processes' in the binding configuration.

 

Step 2: Retrieve the OAuth 2.0 Credentials

After creating the service binding, you need to retrieve the credentials to configure the OAuth 2.0 client in your SAP S/4HANA or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system.

    • Access Instances and Subscriptions — In the SAP BTP cockpit, in the navigation area, choose Services > Instances and Subscriptions.

 

    • Select Service Instance — Under Instances, choose the service instance of the cloud edition. The details screen for the service instance expands on the right side.

 

    • View Credentials — In the details screen, choose a service binding name to open the Credentials screen.

 

  • Retrieve Details — The Credentials screen displays the url, clientId, clientSecret, and drccApiUrl for this service binding.

     

Note: You need these credentials in the next steps when configuring the OAuth 2.0 client.

Summary
You have now successfully created a service binding using OAuth 2.0 client authentication in SAP BTP and retrieved the credentials needed for the integration. The next step is to use the clientId, clientSecret, url, and drccApiUrl to configure the OAuth 2.0 client in your SAP S/4HANA or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system, which will enable secure communication with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition.

Step 3: Downloading the Server Root Certificate

You use the server root certificate to ensure server authenticity between SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition and your business system. The server root certificate proves the authenticity of the server running the cloud edition.

Note: The steps in this section are for Chrome Browser.

  • Access the drccApiUrl: Open a browser and paste the drccApiUrl from the service binding into the address field. Your browser may display an "Unauthorized" or a "404 Not Found" message, but you can still complete the indicated steps.

     

 

  • Open Security Settings: In the address field, find the connection security settings of your browser. A dialog box with security information opens.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  • View Certificate Details: In the dialog box, look for an option related to Connection or Security and choose it. Then, look for an option related to Certificate and choose it. A new window opens with the certificate details.

  • Select Root Certificate: In the new window, search for a view that displays the certificate and its root certificates as parents in a hierarchical structure. The view might be hidden in a different tab named Details, Certification Path, Certification Authority, or something similar. Choose the server root certificate that is the top certificate in the hierarchical structure (e.g., ISRG Root X1). Export and save the server root certificate in .crt format.

Step 4: Adding a Server Root Certificate to the Certificate Trust List

Add the server root certificate to the certificate trust list of your business system so that the system trusts SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition to exchange information.

  • Access Trust Manager: In your business system, access the Trust Manager (STRUST) transaction
  • Change Mode: Switch to edit mode.
  • Select PSE: Choose the SSL Client (Anonymous) PSE.

 

  • Import Certificate: In the Certificate section, choose Import Certificate and add the server root certificate.

 

  • Update List: Choose Add to Certificate List and Save your entries.

entry.title: New ways in S4HANA to enhance or add input fields in the Transportation Cockpit using Field Catalog
entry.content:
Introduction:

In the SAP S/4HANA 2023 release for SAP TM onwards, new functionality is now available: Field Catalogs for the Transportation Cockpit. Using this feature, you can add custom fields, bring standard fields to the cockpit, hide/unhide fields, change properties, and set up a variant for the Transportation Cockpit(TC), setting up a sequence of which fields will be displayed. This feature does not require any enhancements to the Web Dynpro Component Configuration. 

This blog will illustrate all the configurations and steps on how to add editable custom fields to the Transportation Cockpit and save data to the TOR root.

Explanation:

First, the custom fields were added to the TOR Root Note Extension Structure - /SCMTMS/INCL_EEW_TOR_ROOT

Then go to Fiori Tile in your development/customizing client 

In the Define Field Catalogs for TC tile, you will see all standard field catalogs available for TC. Usually, we copy the standard field catalog to the Z custom based on the requirement and make configuration changes to the Z custom catalog. Once changes are done, the same Z custom field catalog is configured in Page Layouts for the Transportation Cockpit Tile. 

In our case, I am using Road Freight Orders, Content object = 0033, Z field catalog created. 

Navigating to the details in the Field Catalog, the screen below is displayed, showing the generated maximum structure, the user interface structure, and all available fields 

Now you see the Maximum User Interface Structure in this case - /SCMTMS/S_TC_M_0033. And the same zz custom fields were already available in this structure, as had been added to the append structure TOR extension include earlier.

Refresh the field catalog page you will see these custom fields in the section below:

Check the Display (Default) for the custom fields and sequence accordingly, and save. It will add the fields to the TC. 

Make sure the Z field catalog is mapped to page layouts as below:

TC displaying with custom fields:

If the custom fields are non-editable and for display only purposes, no further changes are required; the following steps can be ignored.

To make the custom fields editable in the TC. Below, the Class method was implemented. 

Class - /SCMTMS/CL_UI_PLN_UIBB_BASE

Method - GET_ENHANCEMENT_FIELD_DESCR

In the RETURNING structure of this method for each custom field, the display type was set to input field display_type   = sc_enhancement_display_types-input_field 

A bit of debugging will help for sure. And you will get all the custom fields in this method in the IMPORT structure to change the field display properties.

Once you launch the TC now, the custom fields will be input and editable. 

Next Step: data saving and setting the UI controller TAG to identify a change in the editable field

Since it was a Road Freight Order List in TC, the following class and method were enhanced.

/SCMTMS/CL_UI_VIEWEXIT_PLN_NEW

Method - /BOFU/IF_FBI_VIEW_EXITINTF_RUN~ADAPT_CHANGE_LOG

CT_CHANGE_LOG below will have all the new values and old values of the fields changed in the TC.

For data passing to the TOR mass structure for the custom fields, the structure below was enhanced. Also, the same field name was added, and the _CHGD flag field was added for each custom field. This is mandatory. 

Once the above structure is enhanced, in the method - /BOFU/IF_FBI_VIEW_EXITINTF_RUN~ADAPT_CHANGE_LOG, we need to populate the lt_tor_massupd structure with the input data for the custom field and set 'X' for the _CHGD field as below. 

Then set the UI controller TAG to identify a change in an editable field;

Now all the steps are completed. Test the changes; it should work seamlessly. 

Conclusion:

With this approach, there were no changes made in the WDA/FPM component. Using the same approach, other Transportation Cockpit Lists and Hierarchies can be extended/enhanced.

I hope this blog information is helpful. Please drop a comment for any feedback/suggestions/questions.

entry.title: Why SAP Document AI Matters More Than Most Enterprises Think
entry.content:
Introduction

When everyone gets excited about the same topics, conversations will naturally center around them. Eventually, that excitement will catch on, and you’ll find yourself on the same wavelength as everyone else. Currently, as businesses focus on enterprise AI projects, most discussions revolve around similar themes: copilot, agents, and large language models. However, despite significant investments in AI, many businesses still face a common operational obstacle: documents.

Regardless of the industry, invoices, purchase orders, supplier forms, shipping documents, contracts, and compliance documents flow between departments daily. Each requires some degree of manual verification or validation and often involves multiple systems or workflows. This is where SAP Document AI comes in.

SAP Document AI transforms unstructured business documents into structured operational data, helping businesses modernize their operations. Instead of just digitizing your documents, you can use Document AI to make them useful and functional within your business. Even with advancements in intelligent front-end experiences, the foundational infrastructure supporting these solutions still relies on manual verification, various data extraction methods, and disconnected workflows. Document intelligence within SAP environments has matured, and companies now view it as a key automation initiative rather than a secondary option.

Our Experience

Adopting Document AI can reduce the need for labor to support business functions, leading to more efficient solutions. SAP Document AI addresses a common challenge in enterprise environments: extracting, understanding, and organizing information from business documents. In many companies, important operational data arrives in formats like PDFs, scanned files, emails, invoices, and forms, which ERP systems struggle to process without manual help.

SAP Document AI lowers this friction by using AI to identify document types, extract relevant fields, classify information, and prepare that data for business workflows. This means organizations can automate many document-heavy processes across finance, procurement, supply chain, HR, and other operational areas, rather than having employees manually review documents and transfer information between systems. The value of using Document AI goes beyond faster document processing; it empowers operational workflows to operate with greater speed, consistency, and scalability. As enterprises manage more operational data, minimizing manual document reliance becomes essential for maintaining efficiency.

We had the opportunity to see this first-hand. In this scenario, the company was operating in the oil and gas industry, where customer representatives and field engineers constantly generate operational documents while working across remote locations. Every service ticket, carrier document, export filing, client approval, invoice, or credit memo needed to be attached to the correct Sales Order inside SAP for compliance and operational traceability purposes. The challenge was that these documents arrived through emails in different languages, layouts, and formats, often with little context beyond a subject line. As a result, employees had to manually open every file, identify what type of document it was, locate the Sales Order reference somewhere inside the content, rename the file according to internal naming conventions, and upload it into SAP. At a scale of more than 8,000 documents per month, this translated into over 1,000 hours of manual effort every month, distributed across teams in multiple regions.

The organization already had partial automation in place through legacy capture tools and batch jobs, but the process still depended heavily on human intervention before the automation could even begin. A single typo in a file name or an incorrectly classified document could silently break the workflow and prevent the document from being linked correctly inside SAP. By implementing SAP Document AI on SAP BTP, the process shifted from manual interpretation to intelligent document processing. Incoming documents were automatically picked up through SAP Integration Suite, classified using SAP Document AI, and processed to extract key operational data such as Sales Order numbers, vendor details, amounts, and document dates. The extracted information was then validated and routed directly into SAP workflows, significantly reducing manual effort while improving scalability, consistency, and operational visibility across the entire process.

Why This Matters Beyond Document Processing

What made this project valuable was not just the automation itself. I would say it was rather the impact it had on daily operations. Once documents could be processed and validated in a more consistent way, teams spent far less time trying to interpret files manually or fixing small mistakes that slowed everything down. Workflows became easier to manage across different regions, and the process no longer depended so heavily on people knowing specific naming conventions or internal steps that were never formally documented.

Over time, we also realized that SAP Document AI was doing much more than helping with document processing. It became part of the operational structure supporting the entire workflow. As companies continue investing in SAP Business AI and intelligent workflows, having reliable operational data becomes increasingly important. If documents are still disconnected from the systems around them, scaling automation becomes much harder than expected.

Conclusion

As we said at the beginning, a lot of the conversation around enterprise AI focuses on what people can immediately see: copilots, AI agents, chat interfaces, and new user experiences. At the same time, many operational problems still happen behind the scenes, especially in processes where important business information is buried inside emails, PDFs, scanned files, and manual workflows.

Our experience with SAP Document AI showed us that document intelligence is no longer just a secondary automation tool. It can play an important role in helping businesses create cleaner and more scalable operations. When documents become structured operational data instead of isolated files, workflows move faster, teams spend less time on repetitive tasks, and enterprise systems become easier to scale over time.

entry.title: Is SAP Still a Software Company? Reflections from Sapphire 2026
entry.content:
Like many of you, we attended SAP Sapphire Orlando 2026 last week. Before it, our minds were already set on the most likely topics the conversations would revolve around. We thought about the usual when it comes to enterprise AI themes: copilots, new generative capabilities, maybe the launching of something unexpected. Of course, those topics were covered, and we were right to assume that they would be the center of the event. However, the keynote didn’t waste any time and soon revealed that we were there for something much bigger; we were there to witness the first steps towards a bigger strategic shift that is taking place inside SAP. The discussion was no longer just about adding AI features into enterprise software, but about redefining the role ERP systems could play in the next generation of enterprise AI execution.

Over the past two years, most organizations have been implementing copilots that help employees be more productive. For instance, employees can send emails faster, write meeting minutes faster, and look for information easier. However, once enterprises attempt to implement AI on a larger scale, it becomes clear how difficult it is for businesses to actually make things work. Recommendations can be made but putting them into practice in an environment full of disparate systems, workflows, approvals, and data sources can be extremely hard.

This is precisely why SAP's approach to AI seems so unique. Instead of presenting AI as another layer separate from the core of enterprise operations, SAP is attempting to integrate AI into the very systems where business processes take place. As one of the speakers emphasized during their keynote presentation, ERP is "the brain of every company." This idea formed the basis for all the presentations at Sapphire. SAP's view on enterprise AI is that it becomes much more valuable when it operates with process awareness, governance, semantic business context, and operational data.

One of the key announcements made at Sapphire is SAP Business AI Platform. Surprisingly, SAP did not introduce it as "another AI offering". This product is positioned as an architectural layer connecting enterprise AI to operational systems and business processes. The purpose here is to merge ERP process knowledge, Business Data Cloud, AI orchestration, governance frameworks, and semantic business context into a single enterprise AI layer for executing actions, not just giving suggestions.

Another dominant topic at the Sapphire event was moving from AI assistants to operational AI agents. SAP introduced hundreds of AI agents and assistants for Finance, Procurement, Supply Chain, HCM, and Customer Experience processes. However, the number of them was not really the point of their announcement. Much greater attention was paid to orchestration. SAP clearly communicated that enterprise AI will not succeed with isolated copilots, but rather with integrated AI systems that can interact in operational processes. In other words, the real challenge today is not just implementing AI solutions. The goal is to achieve operationalizing them within the enterprise.

Especially fascinating about this approach is its implications for enterprise architecture going forward. Traditionally, enterprises have always treated enterprise software as an assembly of individual platforms that can be integrated. But now, AI is starting to reveal the limitations of such an approach. The more fragmented the operational environment becomes, the less effective it is for AI algorithms to work. This is likely the reason behind SAP’s long focus on topics such as Business Data Cloud, semantic layer, governance models, and process orchestration. It looks like SAP believes that the future success of enterprise AI lies not in how well the individual models perform but rather in the level of their connectivity and operational awareness.

This also signals an important shift for SAP partners and customers. Until recently, discussions around digital transformation revolved around implementation, migration, and modernization. But as enterprise AI becomes more prominent, the focus is shifting to operational readiness. Clean data structures, process standardization, governance models, and ERP systems become prerequisites for successful AI adoption rather than just IT requirements. In a way, Sapphire 2026 suggests that the most capable adopters of AI are not necessarily those experimenting with the greatest number of copilots, but those with the most robust operational foundations to support them.

Following Sapphire 2026, it becomes increasingly clear that SAP tries to evolve beyond being defined as a software company. It seeks to position itself as the operational layer for AI, merging ERP logic, enterprise data, governance, and AI orchestration into a single architecture. If this vision is going to materialize is yet to be seen, but the direction is hard to deny. SAP believes that the next wave of competitive advantages in enterprise AI does not lie in the strength of the AI models but in the ownership of the operational systems that execute AI-driven business decisions.

entry.title: Transformation Journey with IFRS 18
entry.content:
IFRS 18: The new Accounting Gold Standard

The International Accounting Standards Board (IASB) issued IFRS 18 Presentation and Disclosure in Financial Statements in April 2024 to replace IAS 1. IFRS 18 improves the clarity and comparability of financial statements by introducing a mandatory structure for the statement of profit or loss. It requires entities to classify income and expenses into five defined categories - operating, investing, financing, income taxes, and discontinued operations while also introducing new disclosure requirements for management defined performance measures. The standard is effective for annual reporting periods beginning on or after 1 January 2027, with early adoption permitted and retrospective application required.

How does IFRS 18 impact Customers?

For customers, IFRS 18 will impact multiple areas in their current system set-up and would typically require adjustments to, but not limited to - chart of accounts design, G/L account assignments, financial statement version structure, updated cash flow statement, changes in group reporting configurations and to generate comparative financial statement version for previous year.

While S/4HANA’s Universal Journal provides a solid foundation to accommodate these changes, customers will need to proactively review configurations, calculations, and governance to ensure compliant and consistent reporting across entities. This will require early and proactive engagement with auditors to support the timely adaptation of the organization’s accounting policies in accordance with IFRS 18.

IFRS 18 should be approached as a business transformation initiative, not an IT project. For customers, it drives significant changes to financial reporting processes, data models, and governance frameworks, with IT enabling these changes rather than leading them.

Transition to IFRS 18

Customers should treat the adoption of IFRS 18 in an SAP Cloud ERP, SAP Cloud ERP, Private environments as transformation project. A typical IFRS 18 Transformation project could look as below:

We will summarize the steps for each phase but not limited of this transformation journey to in the below section.

Discover Phase

This is one of the most important phases for IFRS 18 Transformation. Some of the key activities that could be executed in Discover Phase.

Early Alignment with Auditors:

The adoption of IFRS 18 will significantly impact the organization’s financial reporting, including the requirement to present FY 2026 and FY 2027 comparatives under IFRS 18 despite prior audits. Early engagement with auditors is therefore critical to align on interpretation and application.

Key focus areas should include updates to the Chart of Accounts, general ledger structure and posting logic, alignment on the treatment of foreign exchange gains and losses, and an auditor‑aligned approach to restating or reclassifying prior‑year information to ensure comparability.

Understand Different Options Available for P&L Classification as per IFRS 18:

The introduction of IFRS 18, with its required classification of income and expenses into Operating, Investing, and Financing categories, will necessitate a thoughtful review of the Chart of Accounts (COA) and may require more granular GL accounts to enable accurate and consistent mapping. A clear understanding of how P&L accounts are posted and the underlying nature of the transactions should drive their appropriate categorization under IFRS 18, thereby reducing judgement, manual adjustments, and classification inconsistencies

For more information, refer to the Help Documentation

SAP Cloud ERP, Private: Activating Cost of Sales Accounting

SAP ERP: Activating Cost of Sales Accounting

In line with IFRS 18 requirements for consistent presentation of expenses by function, the functional area can be automatically derived in the accounting document through appropriate assignment within both process-related configurations and relevant master data.

For more information, refer to the Help Documentation:

SAP Cloud ERP, Private: Deriving Functional Area

SAP Cloud ERP: Deriving Functional Area

SAP ERP: Deriving Functional Area

Organizations already using Cost of Sales Accounting shall ensure that any revision to functional areas aligns with IFRS sub‑categorization requirements while continuing to support all existing statutory, management, and operational reporting needs without disruption.

Financial Statement Versions & Cash Flow Statement (Indirect Method):

IFRS 18 introduces new mandatory subtotals, such as Operating Profit, requiring existing Financial Statement Versions (FSVs) to be updated or replaced to align with the new five-category structure.

These changes affect global hierarchies used in Fiori reporting. Using the Manage Global Hierarchies app, customers can maintain FSV hierarchies based on G/L accounts, functional areas, or a combination of both, to support IFRS 18–compliant reporting.

Depending on the SAP release version, certain customers are able to configure Financial Statement Versions (FSVs) in a way that supports IFRS 18–compliant reporting requirements

SAP would deliver a sample Financial Statement Version, which will be released as specified in the below notes:

SAP Cloud ERP, Private: 3696338 - Advisory Note on IFRS 18 Transition in S/4HANA Private Cloud, S/4HANA - SAP for Me

SAP Cloud ERP: 3694359 - How to Adopt IFRS 18 for Financial Reporting in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

SAP would also provide a new Fiori Application for Cash Flow statement (Indirect Method) for SAP ERP Cloud, Private. Please refer to the Roadmap Explorer for more details.

Scope items 1GA will also be updated to include reference content for IFRS 18, which can be adapted as per customer requirements across SAP Cloud ERP and SAP Cloud ERP, Private.

Treatment of Foreign Exchange Gain or Loss:

According to IFRS 18, foreign currency exchange gains and losses must be presented in separate categories. The category of FX Gain/Loss is decided by the nature of the items from which gain or loss arise. For example:

  • FX Gain/loss arising from Trade Receivables/Payables --> Operating Category
  • FX Gain/loss arising from Cash and Cash equivalents --> Investing Category
  • FX Gain/loss arising from Issued bonds, borrowings --> Financing Category

As part of the IFRS 18 transformation, the nature of postings to each GL account must be analysed to determine whether a GL requires bifurcation to avoid mixed transaction types and ensure compliant presentation. Where new GL accounts are introduced, their foreign exchange gain or loss treatment must be reassessed to ensure accurate currency remeasurement. Accordingly, foreign currency valuation configuration must be adjusted based on whether the classic or advanced foreign currency valuation approach is being used.

When advanced foreign currency valuation is activated, valuation rules can include multiple steps, each using semantic tags to classify monetary items and assign the corresponding unrealized gain or loss accounts.

If you continue to use Classic Foreign Currency Valuation and do not plan to migrate to Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation prior to IFRS 18 adoption, you may update the unrealized gain/loss G/L account for transaction key KDF using transaction code OB09

Postings generated through Treasury & Risk Management (TRM):

Treasury and Risk Management (TRM) generate diverse profit and loss items that must be accurately classified as operating, investing, or financing to comply with IFRS 18.  For further details on TRM, refer to the SAP community blog post:  

IFRS 18 Compliance for Treasury and Risk Management in SAP Cloud ERP: Key Impacts and Approaches

Group Reporting:

For more details on how Group Reporting can support your IFRS 18 adoption journey, please refer to SAP Community blog post: 

IFRS 18 and SAP S/4HANA Group Reporting: What It M... - SAP Community.

Comparative Financial Statement Version:

Because IFRS 18 requires comparative statement, the 2026 data (comparatives for 2027) must be reportable in the new format. For more details, please refer to blog:

IFRS 18 Compliance: Generating Comparative P&L Rep... - SAP Community

Prepare Phase

In the context of an IFRS 18 transformation project, once the impacted reporting and finance areas are identified during the Discover phase, the Prepare phase involves setting up the project plan, identifying the relevant business and IT teams, and provisioning a sandbox environment to execute and validate the required changes.

Create a Plan:

Create a concise, IFRS 18–aligned critical‑path plan covering Chart of Accounts (COA) redesign, Cost of Sales activation (where applicable), Financial Statement Version (FSV) updates, and Foreign Exchange reconfiguration to enable the new presentation, aggregation, and disclosure requirements.

Identify Teams:

An IFRS 18 transformation requires coordinated involvement of Project Management, Accounting Policy Owners, Controllers, Finance Operations and FP&A, Management Performance Measure owners, IT and Reporting systems teams, Audit and Controls (internal and external) and Change and Training teams to ensure compliant financial statement presentation, robust disclosures, system readiness, and clear stakeholder communication.

Provision for a sandbox environment:

Create and maintain a sandbox environment using a recent copy of production data to enable early prototyping.

Explore Phase

This is the most critical phase of this project. Key activities that should be executed in Explore Phase.

Mapping COA to IFRS Categories:

Map every existing P&L account to the mandated IFRS 18 categories: Operating, Investing, Financing, Income tax and discontinued operations. Assignment of a P&L account to a particular category will depend on the nature of transaction from which this posting is generated.

As part of the IFRS 18 transformation, when Cost of Sales accounting is activated, the required functional areas must be clearly defined together with robust and transparent derivation rules to ensure consistent classification of expenses by function.

List down any GAP identified during the mapping exercise.

Create/Update of Financial Statement Version (FSVs) and Cash Flow Statement (Indirect Method):

As part of the IFRS 18 transformation, identify and agree the new Financial Statement Version (FSV) format with the auditor, ensuring clear segregation of operating, investing, financing, tax, and discontinued operations categories. The FSV structure should leverage functional areas, where available, and align with the new sample FSVs and the Cash Flow Statement (Indirect Method) app introduced during the Discover phase to ensure compliance and consistency across financial statements.

FX Gain/Loss Changed due to bifurcation of underlying GL accounts:

If the Chart of Accounts (COA) are going to have new set of GLs based on nature of postings, Foreign currency valuation configuration will also be changed. List down all the GLs for which Foreign currency valuation configuration needs to be updated. Define the posting rules based on advance or classis FC valuation approach you are using.

Comparative Financial Statement Version:

IFRS requires comparative statements to be presented with 2026 data restatement. Please refer to the blog:

IFRS 18 Compliance: Generating Comparative P&L Rep... - SAP Community

Thereafter, decide the approach to be taken. Once finalized, you may need to add additional extension ledger or other solution based on your ERP version and the approach chosen.

Document the Design:

After finishing the mapping files, make a simple design document. In this document, list each area clearly, such as GL requirements, the new FSV structure, cash flow changes, and FX impacts.

At this stage, you should also create a list of gaps where the standard solution is not enough to meet your needs.

Realize Phase (Configuration & Testing)

During the Realization phase, we will implement the approved design by configuring the system, creating required master data, developing custom enhancements (if needed), and testing the solution. This work will support the new IFRS 18 presentation and disclosure requirements, especially around income statement structure, operating categories, and cash flow reporting.

We will create new GL accounts and update the Chart of Accounts, ensuring correct assignment to account determination settings. Cost of Sales Accounting will be configured, and the required functional areas will be defined and carefully derived to support IFRS 18‑aligned income statement presentation.

A new Financial Statement Version (FSV) will be created using transaction OB58 or the Manage Global Hierarchies Fiori app to reflect the updated IFRS 18 structure (operating, investing, and financing categories).

The Fiori Cash Flow Statement (Indirect Method) app will be activated, or a custom report will be developed if defined in the Discover Phase, to support enhanced cash flow disclosures under IFRS 18.

Where transaction-level detail is not available, new or updated allocation rules will be configured to reclassify amounts from one GL account to multiple reporting GLs, ensuring correct IFRS 18 categorisation.

If required, Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation will be configured using valuation rules and semantic tags, or Classic Foreign Currency Valuation (OB09) will be adjusted, in line with reporting requirements.

System Validation (SIT & UAT):

Below are some examples of activities during this phase but not limited to:

  • Validate that the transactions generated are now posted to correct setup of GLs as per nature of transaction.
  • FSV is generated as per required and agreed reporting format.
  • Derivation of functional areas for different processes is correct and complete.
  • Posting of FX Gain/Loss is posted correctly to correct set of GLs
  • Cash Flow Statement (Indirect Method) is working as expected.
  • Simulate month end and quarter end closing to validate that there are no unexpected issues coming.

In this phase active involvement of accounting and finance team is crucial to make the transition successful.

Deploy & Run Phase

Below are some activities but not limited to, that could be considered in this phase:

Cutover Activities:

Some of the cutover activities related to IFRS 18 Transformation Project could be, but not limited to:

  1. Move all required system changes (standard and custom) to the system in the correct order
  2. Create or update basic financial data, such as new GL Accounts, Assignment of Cost Centres to correct functional categories, New Cost Element Groups etc
  3. Switch on the Cash Flow Statement app and verify it works correctly
  4. Check that all automatic accounting rules (derivations and substitutions) are correctly set
  5. Make sure all cost allocations (allocations between departments or functions) are correctly updated
  6. Activate Cost of Sales Accounting so expenses are reported by business function
  7. Update user access and roles so users can see new reports and apps

Conclusion

IFRS 18 is the biggest change to how financial statements are presented since IFRS began. While it does not change how revenue or assets are measured, it significantly changes how income and expenses are grouped and shown in the financial statements.

IFRS 18 applies from 1 January 2027, and companies need to start preparing now to produce comparative information for 2026 and avoid implementation challenges

Credits to Co Author: @Pradeep_M

entry.title: Conversational Analytics on SAP S/4HANA CDS View Data with AI Claude Code and SAP BusinessObjects
entry.content:
Introduction

What if your business users could simply ask a question about their SAP S/4HANA data and get an interactive chart back without writing a single query, opening SAP Analysis for Office, or waiting for a new WebI report to be built?

That is exactly what I built: a prompt-driven analytics agent that sits on top of SAP BusinessObjects (BOBJ) and lets users explore S/4 CDS View data through natural language. In this post I will walk through the architecture, the two AI-powered flows at the heart of it, and what I learned along the way.

The Problem

SAP S/4 CDS View queries contain rich analytical data, but consuming that data typically requires either:

  • A pre-built WebI report — static, and requires BI developer involvement to change, or
  • Direct access to S/4 CDS views via Analysis for Office or other reporting tools — technical, not suitable for casual users.

Business users are stuck waiting. BI analysts are stuck building reports for every new question. This agent breaks that cycle.

What I Built

A browser-based agent - no SAP GUI, no thick client, that:

  • Connects to BOBJ via the REST API using standard username/password
  • Browses your existing WebI reports and lets you pick one as the analytical starting point, just as a base
  • Reads the S/4 CDS View query structure — dimensions, measures, variable prompts — directly from the report's data provider
  • Generates seed prompts using Claude: one clickable question per report block, written in plain business language
  • Answers free-form questions by translating natural language into drill/filter parameters and re-executing the query
  • Renders results as interactive Plotly charts (bar, line, pie, scatter) or HTML tables — all styled in SAP's brand palette

Screen Flow

The agent guides users through four screens from login to live interactive charts. Below are screenshots from a real session using a S/4HANA sflight data report.

Screen 1 — Login

The user enters the BOBJ host URL, authentication type, username and password. The agent calls the BOBJ REST logon endpoint and stores the session token server-side.

 

Figure 1 — Login screen: BOBJ host and credentials

Screen 2 — Report Picker

After login the user browses the BOBJ folder tree. Folders expand on click; .wid Web Intelligence documents are selectable. A preview panel shows the document name and ID before importing.

 

Figure 2 — Report picker: expanding the BOBJ folder tree

 

Figure 3 — A WebI report selected and ready to import

Screen 3a — Variable Prompts

Before the query is executed, the agent reads the report's mandatory and optional variable prompts and renders them as a form. The user fills in values (or leaves optional fields blank) and clicks Run Report.

 

Figure 4 —Variable prompt form: Departure and Arrival Airport filters

Screen 3b — Analytics

Once the report runs, the user lands on the main analytics screen. The left panel shows AI-generated seed prompts (one per report block) and a free-form chat input. The right panel shows the result, initially as a table, then as charts as the user explores.

 

Figure 5 — Initial table result after report execution, with seed prompt generated on the left

 

Figure 6 — Seed prompt panel: AI-generated prompt and free-form chat input

Interactive Charts in Action

Clicking a seed prompt or typing a free-form question triggers the query agent. Claude translates the natural language request into a structured query plan, the data is re-fetched from BOBJ, and Plotly renders the result instantly.

 

Figure 7 — Bar chart: Available Seats by Airline, generated from a seed prompt click

 

Figure 8 — Pie chart: Maximum Economy Capacity by Airline, from a follow-up question

Architecture

The stack is intentionally lean. All data access goes through BOBJ's REST API — the agent never connects to SAP S/4 directly. The queries are executed by BOBJ internally, which means existing security, authorizations, and variable prompts all work exactly as they do in WebI.

 

Browser (htmx + Plotly.js)
    │
    ▼
FastAPI Python App
    ├── bobj_client.py   →  SAP BOBJ REST API (Raylight)
    ├── import_agent.py  →  Claude API  (seed prompt generation)
    ├── query_agent.py   →  Claude API  (NL → query plan)
    ├── renderer.py      →  Plotly charts + HTML tables
    └── server.py        →  FastAPI routes + session state

 

The UI is a single HTML file using htmx for server-driven updates and vanilla JS for screen transitions, no frontend framework, no build step.

Two AI Flows

Flow 1 — Import: Turning a Report into Seed Prompts

When a user selects a WebI report, the agent calls four BOBJ REST endpoints to extract the block structure, Data provider dimensions and measures (with technical names and labels), and the Variable prompts.

 

This metadata is sent to Claude with a strict system prompt: generate one seed prompt per report block in plain business language, using only the dimensions and measures available in the schema.

 

For the flight data report, Claude produced:

  • Seed Prompt 1 — "Show Available Seats by Airline as Bar Chart"
  • Seed Prompt 2 — "Show the Maximum Capacity by Airline as Pie Chart"

 

These are not hardcoded — they are generated fresh from whatever report the user picks.

Flow 2 — Query: Natural Language to Parameters

Whether the user clicks a seed prompt or types their own question, the same flow runs:

  • Claude receives the user message, the CDS View schema (dimension and measure tech names + labels), active variable values, and the last 20 conversation turns.
  • Claude outputs a structured JSON query plan specifying data provider, filters, drilldown dimensions, measures, and chart type.
  • The server validates every dimension and measure tech name against the cached schema before touching BOBJ. If Claude hallucinated a field name, a one-time retry sends the error back for self-correction.
  • The validated plan is applied via BOBJ REST API and the result is rendered as a Plotly chart.

 

An example query plan output from Claude:

{
  "data_provider": "DP1",
  "filters": [
    {"dimension": "0DEPAP", "operator": "eq", "value": "FRA"}
  ],
  "drilldown": ["0AIRLINE"],
  "measures": ["AVAILABLE_SEATS"],
  "chart_type": "bar",
  "summary": "Available seats grouped by airline for flights departing Frankfurt."
}

 

The conversation is stateful, follow-up questions like 'Show as a pie chart instead' or 'Filter to Lufthansa only' carry full history and refine the previous query without starting over.

Key Engineering Decisions

  • Technical names over labels —  SAP technical names are the key to reliable LLM queries. Passing both tech_name and label to Claude — and requiring it to output only tech_name — dramatically reduces hallucination. The model maps 'airline' in the user's question to 0AIRLINE in the schema without guessing.
  • Variable scope injection —  Variable values must be part of every query context. If the user ran the report filtered to Frankfurt, every subsequent query includes that filter automatically. Injecting them as 'active variable filters' in the system prompt ensures consistent, scoped results.
  • The ## cell problem —  The BOBJ REST API returns ## for cells where the value is too large to display in the current drill state. The renderer detects and gracefully handles these rather than charting a string as a number.
  • Security by design —  No S/4 direct connection, no RFC. BOBJ's Raylight API is the only integration point, which means all existing S/4 authorizations and security apply automatically.

Project Structure

bobj-s4cds-agent/
  bobj_client.py      # BOBJ REST API client
  import_agent.py     # Seed prompt generation (Claude)
  query_agent.py      # NL → Query plan (Claude)
  renderer.py         # Plotly charts + HTML tables
  server.py           # FastAPI routes + session state
  static/
    index.html        # Single-page UI (htmx + Plotly.js)
  tests/
    test_bobj_client.py
    test_import_agent.py
    test_query_agent.py
    test_renderer.py
    test_server.py
  requirements.txt

 

To run locally:

pip install -r requirements.txt
export BOBJ_HOST=https://your-bobj-host
export ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=https://your-hai-proxy/anthropic/
export ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN=your-token
uvicorn server:app --reload

What's Next

This is v1 and deliberately scoped. Things could be added:

  • SAP SSO / SAML authentication — so users don't need a separate BOBJ password
  • Multi-data-provider joins — combine dimensions from different CDS Views in one chart
  • Saved queries / bookmarks — let users save useful prompts for quick reuse
  • Scheduled delivery — email a chart to stakeholders on a schedule

Closing Thoughts

The core idea here is simple: the CDS View query and its metadata are already a well-structured schema. An LLM is very good at mapping natural language to a structured schema. Connect the two with a thin validation layer and you get a surprisingly capable analytics interface — one that feels natural to business users and does not require a BI developer to answer every new question.

 

If you are working with SAP BOBJ and S/4 or BW, I hope this gives you a useful starting point. I hope you find this article informative and interesting.

entry.title: SAP EWM Migration to S/4HANA: Advanced Capabilities for Carve-Out and Consolidation Scenarios
entry.content:
SAP EWM on Business Suite to SAP EWM on SAP S/4HANA: A proven solution, Now even more powerful

As organizations accelerate their journey to SAP S/4HANA, warehouse management systems face a critical challenge: how to migrate without disrupting daily operations. The standard SAP Migration Cockpit approach requires closing all EWM transactional data and new start on EWM on S/4HANA  - a significant constraint that can extend project timelines and impact business continuity.

In 2020, SAP's Center of Expertise for EWM (CoE EWM) and the Data Management and Landscape Transformation (DMLT) developed an innovative transactional data migration approach specifically designed for customers who did not wish a complete reimplementation of existing EWM solution. This solution enables the migration of an entire classic EWM system to EWM on S/4HANA, including all related transaction data regardless of status. Learn more about the original approach here.

Since its introduction, this solution has been successfully deployed in over 5+ production environments across industries including retail, manufacturing, and logistics (2020-2024).

Now, as of 2025, we're taking this solution further.

What's New: Advanced Migration Capabilities

The proven migration approach has been enhanced by MAX (fka. CoE) and DMLT to support three powerful new scenarios that go beyond basic system-to-system migration:

  • Carve-out: Selective warehouse extraction
  • Consolidation: Merging into existing systems
  • Hybrid: Combining both strategies
